mcqs.question: Which memory management technique divides physical memory into fixed-size blocks called frames?
mcqs.explanation: Paging divides both physical memory into fixed-size frames and logical memory into pages of the same size. This eliminates external fragmentation but may cause internal fragmentation.

mcqs.question: What type of fragmentation is eliminated by paging?
mcqs.explanation: Paging eliminates external fragmentation because pages can be placed in any available frame. However, internal fragmentation still exists because frames are fixed-size.

mcqs.question: In which page replacement algorithm is the page replaced that has been in memory the longest time?
mcqs.explanation: FIFO (First In First Out) maintains pages in a queue and replaces the oldest page (the one that has been in memory the longest) when a page fault occurs.

mcqs.question: What happens during a page fault?

options.text: The requested page is not in memory

mcqs.explanation: A page fault occurs when a process tries to access a page that is not currently in physical memory, requiring the OS to load the page from secondary storage.

mcqs.question: What is the main advantage of virtual memory?

options.text: Allows execution of programs larger than physical memory
Removes the need for an operating system

mcqs.explanation: Virtual memory allows programs larger than physical memory to execute by using disk space as an extension of RAM, providing the illusion of a larger memory.

mcqs.question: Which data structure stores the mapping between virtual addresses and physical addresses in paging?
mcqs.explanation: The page table is a data structure that stores the mapping (translation) between virtual page numbers and physical frame numbers.

mcqs.question: What is thrashing primarily caused by?

options.text: Excessive page faults and constant swapping

mcqs.explanation: Thrashing occurs when the system spends more time in page replacement than executing processes, typically due to having too many processes competing for too few frames.

mcqs.question: In the Optimal page replacement algorithm, which page is replaced on a fault?

options.text: The page that will not be used for the longest time in future

mcqs.explanation: The Optimal algorithm replaces the page that will not be referenced for the longest time in the future. It is theoretical but used as a benchmark for evaluating other algorithms.

mcqs.question: What is the working set of a process?
mcqs.key: The set of pages currently in memory that the process is actively using

options.text: All pages belonging to the process
The set of pages currently in memory that the process is actively using

mcqs.explanation: The working set is the set of pages that a process has referenced in the recent past. Keeping the working set in memory minimizes page faults.

mcqs.question: Which technique allows parent and child processes to share memory initially without copying?
mcqs.explanation: Copy-on-write allows parent and child processes to initially share the same memory pages. The pages are copied only when one process modifies them.

mcqs.question: What is the Belady's Anomaly related to?

options.text: FIFO page replacement algorithm showing more faults with more frames

mcqs.explanation: Belady's Anomaly shows that in some cases, the FIFO algorithm can have more page faults when using more memory frames, which is counter-intuitive.

mcqs.question: What type of address translation does paging use?
mcqs.explanation: Paging uses logical (or virtual) to physical address translation. The logical address is divided into a page number and offset.

mcqs.question: Which memory allocation method suffers from external fragmentation?
mcqs.explanation: Variable partitioning (contiguous allocation) suffers from external fragmentation as free memory gets scattered in small holes between allocated partitions.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the dirty bit in a page table entry?
mcqs.key: To indicate whether a page has been modified since being loaded into memory

options.text: To indicate whether a page has been modified since being loaded into memory
To mark pages as valid or invalid
To store the access time of the page

mcqs.explanation: The dirty bit (or modified bit) indicates whether a page has been modified. If set, the page must be written back to disk before being evicted.

mcqs.question: What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)?

options.text: A hardware cache for address translations

mcqs.explanation: TLB is a special associative cache that stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations to speed up memory access.

mcqs.question: In demand paging, when are pages loaded into memory?
mcqs.explanation: In demand paging, pages are loaded into memory only when a page fault occurs, meaning when a process tries to access a page not currently in memory.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a page replacement algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: FCFS (First Come First Serve) is a CPU scheduling algorithm, not a page replacement algorithm. FIFO is the page replacement algorithm often confused with FCFS.

mcqs.question: What is segmentation in memory management?

options.text: Dividing memory into variable-sized segments based on logical divisions
Swapping processes in and out of memory

mcqs.explanation: Segmentation divides memory into variable-sized segments based on logical divisions of a program like code, data, stack, and heap segments.

mcqs.question: What happens when all frames in memory are occupied and a page fault occurs?

options.text: A page must be evicted to make room for the new page

mcqs.explanation: When all frames are occupied, the OS must select a page to evict (using a page replacement algorithm) to make room for the required page.

mcqs.question: The effective access time (EAT) in paging depends on which factor?

options.text: TLB hit rate, memory access time, and TLB access time

mcqs.explanation: EAT = (TLB access time + memory access time) × hit rate + (TLB access time + 2 × memory access time) × miss rate. It depends on all three factors.
